raw.regions_all: Suriye; Irak; Mısır; Kuzey Afrika; İspanya; Horasan; Mâverâünnehir; Sind; Ermenistan; Kafkasya
raw.narrative_en: The Umayyad Caliphate transformed the Islamic state from a tribal republic into a centralized empire spanning the Mediterranean to Central Asia. The Damascus-based dynasty, founded by Mu'awiya, consolidated state identity under Abd al-Malik by making Arabic the official language and minting Islamic coinage. While conquests reached Spain and Sind, the mawali grievances and Ahl al-Bayt opposition deepened the regime's legitimacy crisis.

raw.parent_tribe: Quraysh - Hāshim (Hz. Peygamber'in amcası al-'Abbās'ın soyundan)
raw.regions_all: Irak; İran; Suriye; Mısır; Hicaz; Horasan; Mâverâünnehir (başlangıçta); Kahire (sonraki dönem)
raw.narrative_en: The Abbasids built the golden age of Islamic civilization and remained the symbolic center of the Sunni world for five centuries. They founded Baghdad, transforming it into the era's greatest intellectual and commercial hub where the Bayt al-Hikma translated Greek, Indian, and Persian knowledge into Arabic. The reigns of Harun al-Rashid and al-Ma'mun represented the zenith, while from the 10th century real power passed to military dynasties like the Buyids and Seljuks. The shadow caliphate in Cairo served a symbolic function, legitimizing Mamluk sultans.

raw.capital: Kahire (Mısır); Şam (Suriye); Halep; Hama; Hıms; Mayyāfāriqīn; Hısn Keyfā
raw.region_primary: Mısır, Suriye, Diyâr-ı Bekr, Cezire, Yemen
raw.regions_all: Mısır; Suriye; Filistin; Diyâr-ı Bekr; Cezire; Yemen; Hicaz
raw.narrative_en: The Ayyubids were the dynasty founded by the Kurdish-origin Saladin after abolishing the Fatimid caliphate, recapturing Jerusalem from the Crusaders at Hattin. Their confederative structure uniting Egypt and Syria symbolized the resurgence of Sunni Islam, though this decentralization eventually led to their fragmentation.

names.es: I Dinastía del País del Mar

Iraqi Shia Sayyid clerical family; founders of SCIRI
Sayyid clerical family; Grand Ayatollah Ali al-Sistani

raw.regions_all: Tebriz; Kazvin; İsfahan; Azerbaycan; Horasan; Irak (geçici); Gürcistan; Herat (geçici)
raw.narrative_en: The Safavids were the dynasty that transformed Iran into a Twelver Shī'ī state, laying the foundations of modern Iranian national identity. Founded through Shāh Ismā'īl's charismatic leadership, the state transformed Isfahan into one of the world's most magnificent capitals under Shāh Abbās. While Ottoman-Safavid rivalry permanently shaped the Sunni-Shī'ī axis of the Middle East, Isfahan's Naqsh-e Jahān Square was built as one of Islamic architecture's most spectacular plazas. The Safavids also institutionalized the Iranian carpet tradition, miniature painting, and Shī'ī theology. The dynasty, which collapsed when the Afghan Ghilzai invasion took Isfahan, permanently determined Iran's political structure.

raw.regions_all: Tebriz; Sultaniye; İsfahan; Bağdat; Azerbaycan; Anadolu (tâbi); Gürcistan; Horasan
raw.successor: Celâyirliler; Muzafferîler; Kurtlar; çeşitli yerel hanedanlar
raw.narrative_en: The Il-Khanids were a Mongol state centered on Iran-Iraq-Azerbaijan founded by Hülegü. Having ended the Abbasid caliphate by destroying Baghdad, the Il-Khanids underwent transformation with Ghāzān Khān's conversion to Islam. Under Öljeitü, they left magnificent architectural works in Sultaniyya, creating the most brilliant example of the Mongol-Islamic synthesis.

raw.regions_all: Bağdat; Tebriz; Sultaniye; Basra
raw.narrative_en: The Jalayirids were a Mongol-Turkic dynasty that rose centered on Baghdad and Tabriz after the Il-Khanid collapse. Sultan Ahmad Jalayir created a rare artist-ruler figure in Islamic art history as both a calligrapher and painter. Severely damaged by Timur's repeated campaigns, the dynasty was liquidated by the Qara Qoyunlu.

raw.regions_all: Tebriz; Van; Erzincan; Bağdat (geçici); Fars (geçici)
raw.narrative_en: The Qara Qoyunlu were a Shī'ī-leaning Turkmen confederation ruling in eastern Anatolia and Azerbaijan. A culturally brilliant period occurred under the poet-ruler Jahānshāh (pen name Ḥaqīqī). They were destroyed by Āq Qoyunlu Uzun Hasan.

raw.regions_all: Diyarbakır; Tebriz; İsfahan; Fars; Irak; Hûzistân
raw.narrative_en: The Āq Qoyunlu were a Turkmen confederation ruling in eastern Anatolia and Iran, becoming a regional power under Uzun Hasan's leadership. Though Uzun Hasan, who sought a Venetian alliance against the Ottomans, was defeated at Otlukbeli, the Āq Qoyunlu legacy directly prepared the ground for the Safavid rise.

raw.regions_all: Hicaz; Suriye; Filistin; Mısır; Irak; İran; Bahreyn; Umman; Yemen
raw.narrative_en: The Rashidun Caliphate represents the founding political order of the Islamic community after Prophet Muhammad's death, dismantling the Sasanian Empire entirely and expelling Byzantium from Syria and Egypt within three decades. Abu Bakr secured Arabian unity through the Ridda Wars, while Umar transformed the conquests into a systematic state apparatus. Uthman standardized the Quranic text, but the civil strife (fitna) under Ali created the defining political fracture in Islamic history.

raw.atlas_name_en_original: The Carmathian or Qarmati Rulers of the line of Abū Sa'īd al-Jannābī
raw.regions_all: al-Ahsā; Bahreyn; Güney Irak (geçici); Suriye sınırı (geçici); Umân (geçici)
raw.narrative_en: The Qarmatians represented the most radical branch of Ismaili Shiism, a militant movement that shook the Islamic world by stealing the Black Stone from the Kaaba. Their egalitarian community model established in eastern Arabia, while controversial, constituted one of the most provocative experiments in Islamic political thought.

raw.notes: Caliphate based in Baghdad then Cairo

raw.regions_all: Horasan; İsfahan; Bağdat; Suriye; Kirmân; Mâverâünnehir (geçici)
raw.narrative_en: The Seljuqs represent one of the most decisive turning points in Islamic history and the founding force of the Turco-Islamic synthesis. Descending from the Qınıq clan of the Oghuz Turks, the Seljuqs defeated the Ghaznavids at the Battle of Dandanaqan (1040), dominated Khurasan, and within fifteen years took Būyid Baghdad, initiating the resurgence of Sunni Islam. Tughril Beg received the title 'Sultan of the East and West' from the Abbasid caliph, permanently institutionalizing the sultanate in Islamic politics. Alp Arslan's victory at Manzikert (1071) opened Anatolia to the Turco-Islamic world, while the era of Malik Shāh and his vizier Niẓām al-Mulk constituted one of the most brilliant administrative periods in Islamic history. The Niẓāmiyya madrasas institutionalized the Islamic educational system, building the intellectual infrastructure of Sunni orthodoxy. Branches established in Iraq, Kirman, Syria, and Anatolia under the Great Seljuq umbrella redrew the political map of the Islamic world.

raw.region_primary: Anadolu → Güneydoğu Avrupa → Ortadoğu → Kuzey Afrika
raw.regions_all: Söğüt/Bursa (erken); İstanbul; Anadolu; Balkanlar; Suriye; Mısır; Hicaz; Irak; Kuzey Afrika; Kırım
raw.narrative_en: The Ottomans established the longest-lasting and largest empire in Islamic history, spanning three continents over six centuries. Rising from a small Anatolian frontier principality to world power, the Ottomans became heirs to the Roman Empire through the conquest of Constantinople (1453) and the political and religious leader of the Islamic world by assuming the caliphate. Reaching the zenith of law, architecture, and military power under Süleyman the Magnificent, the empire created a distinctive civilizational model through Sinan's masterpieces, the kapıkulu system, and the multi-religious millet structure. This dynasty of 36 sultans exited the historical stage after World War I defeat through the National Struggle and the proclamation of the Republic.

raw.regions_all: Mısır; Suriye; Filistin; Hicaz; Kuzey Irak kısmen; Kilikya; Nübya; Kıbrıs (haraçgüzar)
raw.narrative_en: The Mamluks were a dynasty founded by slave-origin military elites that halted the Mongol invasion at Ayn Jalut and completely expelled the Crusaders from Syria. Throughout the Bahri and Burji periods, they positioned Egypt and Syria as the political and cultural center of the Islamic world, developing a unique system where succession passed not from father to son but to the strongest commander.

raw.regions_all: Mısır; Filistin; Suriye; Kuzey Mezopotamya (Cezire)
raw.narrative_en: The Tulunids were the first local dynasty to achieve de facto independence from the Abbasids in Egypt and Syria. The Ibn Tulun Mosque, built by Ahmad ibn Tulun of Turkic origin, still stands in Cairo as one of the oldest and best-preserved examples of Islamic architecture.

raw.regions_all: Musul; Cezire (Kuzey Mezopotamya); Halep; Kuzey Suriye
raw.narrative_en: The Hamdanids ruled in two branches at Mosul and Aleppo, with Sayf al-Dawla transforming his Aleppo court into the era's most brilliant intellectual center. By patronizing figures like al-Mutanabbi and al-Farabi, they left an enduring mark on Islamic cultural history while maintaining the jihad tradition on the Byzantine frontier.

raw.regions_all: Semerkand; Herât; İsfahan; Şîraz; Bağdat (geçici); Hindistan (geçici); Anadolu (geçici)
raw.narrative_en: The Timurids were the dynasty founded by Timur from the Barlas Turco-Mongol clan, transforming Samarkand into a world capital. Timur fundamentally redrew the political map of the Islamic world through military campaigns stretching from China to India, from Russian steppes to the Mediterranean. Yet the true Timurid legacy lies in cultural rather than military achievements: Ulugh Beg's Samarkand observatory broke new ground in astronomy, while the Herat miniature school represents the pinnacle of Islamic art. The era of Husayn Bayqara and Alī Shīr Navā'ī is the golden age of Chagatai Turkish literature. The Timurid cultural legacy was carried to India through the Mughals and to Iran through the Safavids.

raw.parent_tribe: Ukaylî kabilesi (Kuzey Arap / Āmir b. Sa'sa'a)
raw.regions_all: Musul; Cezire (Ceziretülibn Ömer, Nusaybin, Balad); Tikrit; Hit; Ukbara; Ana; Hadise; Kal'atü Ca'ber
raw.narrative_en: The Uqaylids were a Bedouin-origin Shia dynasty ruling Mosul and the Jazira on the Hamdanid legacy. Under Muslim ibn Quraysh, they reached their greatest extent, briefly controlling Aleppo as well. They were the most influential Arab emirate of the Buyid-Seljuk transition period, playing a critical role in the region's political balance.

raw.notes: Deposed in 14 July Revolution 1958; claimant Prince Ra'ad bin Zeid in Jordan

raw.regions_all: Hille; Kufe bölgesi; Orta Irak
raw.narrative_en: The Mazyadids emerged from the Bedouin Asad tribe based in Hilla, establishing a Shia-leaning emirate in central Iraq. Dubays ibn Sadaqa's founding of the city of Hilla and his defiant policy against the Seljuks constituted the dynasty's most notable period.

raw.regions_all: Musul ve çevresi
raw.narrative_en: The Lu'lu'ids were a unique dynasty founded by a commander of Armenian slave origin who governed Mosul alone for thirty years. Badr al-Dīn Lu'lu', as an art patron ruler inheriting the Zangid legacy, preserved Mosul's cultural richness.

raw.regions_all: Horasan (Nîşâbûr); Bağdat (şurta komutanları)
raw.narrative_en: The Tāhirids, a family of Khuzā'a origin who rose through Abbasid service, formed the first effectively independent Iranian dynasty. Contributing to the development of Islamic culture in Khurasan while also running the shurta (police) organization in Baghdad, this dynasty maintained an autonomous administration while remaining loyal to the Abbasids.

raw.regions_all: Hüveyzā; Basra (geçici); Hûzistân
raw.narrative_en: The Musha'sha'ids were an Arab dynasty founded with extreme Shī'ī ideology in the Khuzestan region of southwestern Iran, ruling for 290 years under the protection of marshland geography. They maintained their existence for a long time as Safavid vassals.

raw.regions_all: Şîraz; İsfahan; Kirmân; Basra (geçici)
raw.narrative_en: The Zands were a short-lived dynasty of Lur origin that established dominance in southern Iran under Karīm Khān's leadership. Using the title 'Wakīl al-Dawla' (regent of the state) instead of 'Padishāh,' Karīm Khān made Shiraz his capital, maintaining a populist and relatively peaceful governance.

raw.regions_all: Bağdat/Irak; Fars (Şîraz); Cibâl (Rey, Hemedân, İsfahan); Kirmân; Hûzistân; Umân (geçici)
raw.narrative_en: The Būyids represent the most paradoxical dynasty in Islamic history, emerging from the Daylam mountains on the Caspian coast to conquer Baghdad and directly control the Abbasid caliph. Rather than destroying the Sunni caliphate, this Shī'ī military power pragmatically utilized it as a legitimacy instrument, creating a unique model in Islamic political tradition. Governing in five branches across Fars, Iraq, Jibal, and Kirman, they maintained dominance for over a century. Transplanting the Daylamī military tradition to the heart of the Islamic world, they also played a pioneering role in literary and scientific patronage.

raw.regions_all: Musul; Halep; Şam; Sincar; Cezîre; Şehrizor
raw.narrative_en: The Zangids were a Turkic dynasty that rose from Seljuq governorship and pioneered the jihad movement against the Crusaders in the Islamic world. Ruling in five branches across Mosul, Aleppo, Damascus, and the Jazira, Nūr al-Dīn Maḥmūd was the most important Zangid ruler who transformed jihad ideology into a systematic state policy. Saladin rose through Nūr al-Dīn's service, inheriting the Zangid legacy.

raw.regions_all: Yezd
raw.narrative_en: The Atabegs of Yazd were a dynasty of Daylamī origin continuing the Kākūyid legacy as a small local principality in Yazd. They were taken over by the Muzaffarids during the Il-Khanid period.

raw.regions_all: Erbil ve çevresi
raw.narrative_en: The Begtiginids were a Turkic dynasty that established an atabegate in Erbil as Zangid vassals and played a significant role in the struggle against the Crusaders. Muzaffar al-Dīn Gökbörü became renowned for fighting alongside Saladin and as the greatest patron of the Mawlid celebration tradition.

raw.regions_all: Rûyân; Kucûr; Nûr
raw.narrative_en: The Bādūspānids ruled for approximately nine centuries in the Rūyān region of western Tabaristan, forming one of the Islamic world's longest-lasting dynasties. This mountain principality, with pre-Sasanian Iranian roots, witnessed the rise and fall of numerous empires thanks to the advantage of geographic isolation.

raw.regions_all: Luristan; İzec; Hûzistân (kısmen)
raw.narrative_en: The Hazāraspids were a Kurdish/Lur atabegate that ruled in Greater Luristan for over 270 years. As one of the rare dynasties that survived as Il-Khanid vassals, the Hazāraspids maintained their local authority in Luristan's mountainous geography.

raw.regions_all: Ahlat; Erzen; Van Gölü çevresi
raw.narrative_en: The Shāh-i Armanids were a Seljuq frontier principality that consolidated Turco-Islamic presence in the region by centering on the strategic city of Ahlat around Lake Van. Ahlat's position as a major trade center provided the dynasty with economic power, while continuous border conflicts with Armenian and Georgian neighbors persisted.

raw.regions_all: Harran; Edessa (al-Ruhā/Urfa); Sarūj; Raqqa
raw.successor: Selçuklu fethi; ardından Haçlı Edessa Kontluğu
raw.narrative_en: The Numayrids were a small Bedouin emirate ruling in the Harran and Edessa region. Related to the Mirdasids, this dynasty passed into history with the Seljuk advance and the subsequent establishment of the Crusader County of Edessa.

names.en: Hāshimite Sharīfs of Mecca from the 'Awn Family

raw.atlas_name_en_original: The Hāshimite Sharīfs of Mecca from the 'Awn Family
raw.region_primary: Mekke ve Hicaz; ardından Irak ve Ürdün
raw.regions_all: Mekke; Hicaz; Irak (Faysal kolu); Suriye (geçici); Ürdün (Abdullah kolu)
raw.successor: Hicaz'da Suudi fethi (1344/1925); Irak'ta cumhuriyet (1958); Ürdün'de devam
raw.narrative_en: The Hashimite Sharifs of Mecca, as direct descendants of Prophet Muhammad, governed the Hijaz for centuries and shaped the modern Middle East map through the 1916 Arab Revolt. Though they lost the Hijaz to Saudi conquest, they still reign in Jordan.

raw.notes: Arab Shia dynasty of Mosul and Aleppo

raw.regions_all: Diyâr-ı Bekr; Mayyāfāriqīn; Amid (Diyarbakır); Ceziretülibn Ömer
raw.narrative_en: The Marwanids were a Kurdish dynasty established in Diyar Bakr by chiefs exploiting Buyid weakness, representing the region's most important medieval emirate. By developing Silvan and Diyarbakir, they constituted one of the early examples of Kurdish political history.

raw.notes: Kurdish tribal family; KDP and KRG presidency
